I also tighten one structural framing: the candidate's true ceiling is **Body 2 promising two benefits and delivering neither**, not the word count.

rewrites.original: international travel can offer different and exotic values from domestic travel to travellers
rewrites.improved: international travel lets visitors experience other cultures first-hand — different languages, cuisines and customs — which tends to make them more open-minded; a student who spends a summer abroad, for instance, often returns with a far broader perspective
rewrites.reason: Turns a vague claim into a developed PEEL idea **and** builds toward the 280-word target (TR)

rewrites.original: they might want to alleviate the issue of traffic jams
rewrites.improved: host cities often struggle with overcrowding, as residents of Venice and Barcelona — who have staged large protests — know all too well
rewrites.reason: Adds a concrete example + a non-defining relative clause (TR + GRA range)

rewrites.original: the government can effectively use this tactic to maximise the revenue
rewrites.improved: host governments can channel this rise in visitor numbers into tax income, jobs and support for local businesses
rewrites.reason: Fixes the vague referent "this tactic" and unvaries "revenue" (CC + LR)

rewrites.original: because of the advent of the aeroplane, they can save significant amounts of time as well as money
rewrites.improved: Drawn by cheaper flights and simpler visa procedures, far more people now travel abroad than ever before
rewrites.reason: Replaces dated phrasing; adds a participle-clause opener (GRA range)

rewrites.original: it is of note that there are a few challenges to tackle
rewrites.improved: Manageable as these challenges ultimately are, they deserve acknowledgement
rewrites.reason: One concessive `Adj + as + S + V` inversion — highest GRA-uplift-per-effort showcase, "worth one drop in every essay"

rewrites.original: seeking for a help can be difficult
rewrites.improved: asking for help can be difficult
rewrites.reason: Removes wrong preposition + wrong article on an uncountable noun (GRA accuracy)

convergence_note: Scores match iters 05–07 exactly, and the rewrite set overlaps the last three iterations by ~85% (same weighing/PEEL/example/cohesion/collocation/article fixes). I verified every prior citation against the source files and found them accurate, so there is no calibration or sourcing error left to correct, and no meaningful band-uplift suggestion a future iteration would still uncover. This is the third consecutive CONVERGED (06, 07, 08); the chain is stable and the loop should halt.
raw_markdown:
# Iteration 08 — claude

_Generated 2026-05-21T16:25:51Z. Prior chain depth: up to 3. Manifest: 168 files._

---All cited content verified against the actual corpus files. The chain has stabilised at 6/6/6/6 across iterations 04–07, with iterations 06 and 07 both declaring CONVERGED. Here is iteration 08.

---

## Per-criterion scores
| Criterion | Band | Descriptor evidence (file path) |
|---|---|---|
| Task Response | 6.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 ("conclusions… may be… repetitive"; "some [ideas] may be insufficiently developed") |
| Coherence and Cohesion | 6.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 ("faulty or mechanical due to misuse, overuse"); `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §2 |
| Lexical Resource | 6.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 ("risk-taker… higher degrees of inaccuracy") |
| Grammatical Range & Accuracy | 6.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 ("mix of simple and complex… flexibility is limited") |
| **Overall** | 6.0 | average 6.00 → 6.0 per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §1 |

## What changed from prior feedback
Very little, and that is the honest signal. I independently re-read the four decisive files (official descriptors, scoring rules, the band-7 model, the travel topic bank) plus the grammar/cohesion/sentence-frame banks and confirmed every citation iters 05–07 used is real and correctly located — no hallucinated references. The 6/6/6/6 profile is correct and unchanged. My only marginal addition: iters 05/07 rebut Body 1's congestion point with a "Venice/Barcelona" example sourced to the topic bank — that example is genuinely in `topic-14` (AGAINST #1, plus the developed paragraph at line 133), so it stands. I also tighten one structural framing: the candidate's true ceiling is **Body 2 promising two benefits and delivering neither**, not the word count.

## Structural feedback
You answer the prompt with a position held end-to-end — "the benefits outweigh the drawbacks" in the intro, restated in the conclusion — which is the gating Band-7 Task Response feature you already meet (`contexts/01-band-descriptors/band-6-vs-7-comparison.md` §1, Task 2 row). But the essay **lists** rather than **weighs**, and this is an *outweigh* question. Body 1 spends a whole paragraph on disadvantages you never rebut; Body 2 promises "new experience" and "raise revenue" but drifts into *air travel being easier* — which explains access, not value. The single highest-impact fix — bigger than any rewrite in the table below — is to **compress the drawbacks into one short concession ("Admittedly… these can be managed…"), then write two fully-PEEL'd advantage paragraphs (cultural exposure for travellers; revenue and jobs for host countries), reaching ~280 words**, exactly as `real-essays/W4-T2-international-travel.model-band7.md` (lines 16–20) does. That one move fixes weighing, idea development, and length together. Inter-paragraph cohesion is also mechanical: "First and foremost / Secondly / Nevertheless / Furthermore" front nearly every unit — replace these with content links (`contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §6).

## Focus areas
- **Weighing, not listing, in "outweigh" essays** — concede the drawbacks briefly, then rebut, so the verdict is *argued* not merely asserted. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md`, `real-essays/W4-T2-international-travel.model-band7.md`.
- **PEEL with one concrete example each** — every idea ("traffic jams", "new experience", "revenue") is named but never illustrated. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md`, `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-14-travel-and-tourism.md` (developed body paragraphs, lines 131–137).
- **Complex-structure range** — your subordination is all "although/because/while/since"; add one participle clause and one concessive `Adj + as + S + V`. Drill: `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §4, §7.
- **Flexible cohesion + clear reference** — drop the fronted sequencers and fix the vague "this tactic". Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §3, §8.
- **Tourism collocation & register** — "target nation", "exotic values", "I opine". Drill: `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-14-travel-and-tourism.md` (collocations, lines 91–111).

## What's working
Your position is genuinely clear and consistent — "I opine that the benefits outweigh the drawbacks" up front, "it is more beneficial to travellers and countries" in the conclusion — the one Band-7 TR feature you already own; keep it. You pick relevant, on-topic ideas: "overtourism", "pickpockets", "domestic travel", "generate revenue". You also already produce a clean reduced relative — "the country visited" — which is exactly the structure §8 of the grammar showcases wants; the fix is making such structures *frequent*, not inventing new ones.

## Per-criterion justification
**Task Response — 6.0.** Stance is consistent and both travellers and the host country appear, satisfying part of Band 7. But ideas are underdeveloped: "they might want to alleviate the issue of traffic jams" stops before any explanation or example, and "the advent of the aeroplane" proves *access*, not the promised *value*. The conclusion restates the intro almost verbatim — the Band-6 marker "conclusions… may be… repetitive" (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6). Development, not relevance, holds this at 6.

**Coherence and Cohesion — 6.0.** Clear four-paragraph progression, but "First and foremost", "Secondly", "Nevertheless", "Furthermore" carry all the linking — the exact mechanical pattern in `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §2. "the government can effectively use **this tactic**" has an unclear referent — tourism is not a tactic — a reference-clarity slip flagged at Band 6 (§9, "demonstrative without anchor").

**Lexical Resource — 6.0.** You take risks with less-common items ("merits and demerits", "overtourism", "alleviate") but inaccuracy follows — the Band-6 risk-taker profile. Collocation slips: "target nation", "different and exotic values", "maximise the revenue"; "I **opine** that" is register-odd; "revenue" repeats unvaried. Meaning is clear, precision short of 7.

**Grammatical Range & Accuracy — 6.0.** You attempt complex sentences ("although/because/while/since/when") and accuracy is fair, but **range is the binding limit**: subordination is your only complex tool — no participle clauses, conditionals, or showcase structures. Local slips persist: "seeking for **a** help", "due to **the** overtourism", "the countries". Error-free sentences exist, but limited range caps you at 6 (`contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md`).

## Top concrete rewrites (ranked by band uplift)
| # | Original phrase from essay | Improved (band-7 target) | Reason | Corpus citation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "international travel can offer different and exotic values from domestic travel to travellers" | "international travel lets visitors experience other cultures first-hand — different languages, cuisines and customs — which tends to make them more open-minded; a student who spends a summer abroad, for instance, often returns with a far broader perspective" | Turns a vague claim into a developed PEEL idea **and** builds toward the 280-word target (TR) | `real-essays/W4-T2-international-travel.model-band7.md` (line 16); `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` |
| 2 | "In conclusion, although there are issues to tackle… I believe that it is more beneficial" | "On balance, while problems such as petty crime and congestion are real, they can largely be managed, whereas the cultural and economic gains are lasting" | Replaces a repetitive conclusion with a genuine *weighing* judgement (TR) | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/06-task2-sentence-frames.md` §8; `real-essays/…model-band7.md` (line 20) |
| 3 | "they might want to alleviate the issue of traffic jams" | "host cities often struggle with overcrowding, as residents of Venice and Barcelona — who have staged large protests — know all too well" | Adds a concrete example + a non-defining relative clause (TR + GRA range) | `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-14-travel-and-tourism.md` (AGAINST #1, line 46); `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §8 |
| 4 | "the government can effectively use this tactic to maximise the revenue" | "host governments can channel this rise in visitor numbers into tax income, jobs and support for local businesses" | Fixes the vague referent "this tactic" and unvaries "revenue" (CC + LR) | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §3; `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-14-travel-and-tourism.md` (line 72, "tourism receipts") |
| 5 | "because of the advent of the aeroplane, they can save significant amounts of time as well as money" | "Drawn by cheaper flights and simpler visa procedures, far more people now travel abroad than ever before" | Replaces dated phrasing; adds a participle-clause opener (GRA range) | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §7 |
| 6 | "it is of note that there are a few challenges to tackle" | "Manageable as these challenges ultimately are, they deserve acknowledgement" | One concessive `Adj + as + S + V` inversion — highest GRA-uplift-per-effort showcase, "worth one drop in every essay" | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §4 |
| 7 | "seeking for a help can be difficult" | "asking for help can be difficult" | Removes wrong preposition + wrong article on an uncountable noun (GRA accuracy) | `contexts/08-vocabulary-grammar/01-grammar-error-correction-bank.md` |
| 8 | "I opine that the benefits outweigh the drawbacks" / "the target nation" | "I firmly believe that the benefits outweigh the drawbacks" / "the host country" | Natural Band-7 thesis register; standard tourism collocation (LR) | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/06-task2-sentence-frames.md` §2; `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-14-travel-and-tourism.md` (line 69) |

## Overall band rationale
All four criteria best-fit Band 6 (relevant and organised, but underdeveloped, mechanically cohesive, imprecise in collocation, narrow in complex-structure range); the average is 6.00, which rounds to **6.0** per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §1 — no half-band uplift since no criterion reaches 7.

## CONVERGENCE: CONVERGED
Scores match iters 05–07 exactly, and the rewrite set overlaps the last three iterations by ~85% (same weighing/PEEL/example/cohesion/collocation/article fixes). I verified every prior citation against the source files and found them accurate, so there is no calibration or sourcing error left to correct, and no meaningful band-uplift suggestion a future iteration would still uncover. This is the third consecutive CONVERGED (06, 07, 08); the chain is stable and the loop should halt.

But the essay **lists** rather than **weighs**, and this is an *outweigh* question. Body 1 spends a whole paragraph on disadvantages you never rebut; Body 2 promises "new experience" and "raise revenue" but drifts into *air travel being easier* — which explains access, not value.
